title: HAVING Clause
content:
### HAVING Clause

HAVING filters groups created by GROUP BY based on aggregate conditions. It is like WHERE but for groups rather than individual rows.

---

### HAVING Syntax

```sql
SELECT column1, aggregate_function(column2)
FROM table
GROUP BY column1
HAVING condition;
```

### HAVING vs WHERE

| Clause | Filters | Can use aggregates | Applied |
|--------|---------|-------------------|---------|
| WHERE | Individual rows | No | Before GROUP BY |
| HAVING | Grouped results | Yes | After GROUP BY |

### Basic Example

```sql
-- Find departments with more than 5 employees
SELECT department, COUNT(*) AS emp_count
FROM employees
GROUP BY department
HAVING COUNT(*) > 5;
```

### HAVING with Multiple Conditions

```sql
-- Departments with more than 5 employees AND avg salary > 60000
SELECT department,
       COUNT(*) AS emp_count,
       AVG(salary) AS avg_salary
FROM employees
GROUP BY department
HAVING COUNT(*) > 5
   AND AVG(salary) > 60000;
```

### Combining WHERE and HAVING

```sql
-- WHERE filters rows, then GROUP BY groups, then HAVING filters groups
SELECT department, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary
FROM employees
WHERE active = 1  -- Filter active employees first
GROUP BY department
HAVING AVG(salary) > 50000;  -- Then filter departments
```

### ORDER BY with HAVING

```sql
SELECT department, COUNT(*) AS emp_count
FROM employees
GROUP BY department
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3
ORDER BY emp_count DESC;
```

> **Key Point:** Use WHERE to filter individual rows, HAVING to filter groups. You can use both in the same query.
codeExample:
SELECT category,
       COUNT(*) AS product_count,
       AVG(price) AS avg_price
FROM products
WHERE active = 1
GROUP BY category
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 5
ORDER BY avg_price DESC;

quiz.question: When does HAVING filter groups relative to GROUP BY?
